rationale: The citing paper invokes Nunes and Boatwright (2004) in its core literature framing on reference products, specifically as evidence that unrelated prices can influence willingness to pay via reference-price/cost-comparison processes. It then explicitly positions its own contribution as going beyond that prior stream by testing similarly priced hedonic versus utilitarian references and showing effects on self-signaling and donation behavior, not just transaction utility. Although the citation appears in grouped references, its functional role is clear in motivating and positioning the paper's theoretical contribution.
relation_description: The citing paper treats Nunes and Boatwright (2004) as prior work showing incidental/reference prices affect valuation, and extends that line to a new mechanism and domain. It argues that reference products can shift self-signaling utility in charitable giving, not only perceived cost in purchase settings.
